blocks.text: SO3 tests the correctness and adversarial resilience of the DID/VC credential lifecycle, covering the full attack surface defined during threat modeling. Unlike biometric and blockchain results which are measured quantitatively, SO3 results are largely deterministic. Each test either passes (the contract correctly rejects the attack) or fails (an unauthorized state change occurs).

All 22 access control guards were exercised via tailored adversarial transactions against the live-deployed contracts. Each guard maps to a specific attack vector identified during threat modeling: handshake integrity violations, RBAC boundary crossings, cryptographic forgery attempts, lifecycle state abuse, replay and double-spend attacks, and privilege escalation attempts. Every adversarial transaction resulted in an EVM revert: no tested vector effected an unauthorized state change on the deployed ledger.

Cryptographic non-repudiation was tested using EIP-712 typed data signing with the EIP-2 low-s constraint. Three signature classes were submitted to the contract: a valid signature from the authorized credential holder (accepted, nonce incremented), a forged signature from an unauthorized account (rejected), and a malleable signature with flipped s-value to test the EIP-2 low-s enforcement (rejected). The contract correctly discriminates all three cases.

Replay and double-spend protection relies on a per-user monotonic nonce design. Five functional tests were executed, covering consumed voucher reuse, state bypass attempts, timing abuse against the two-phase revocation grace period, race conditions during pending revocation, and rapid double-submission of identical nonces. All attack vectors were correctly blocked, with the two-phase revocation grace period enforced as designed.
